attachment.content:
- update-config: Use this skill to configure the Claude Code harness via settings.json. Automated behaviors ("from now on when X", "each time X", "whenever X", "before/after X") require hooks configured in settings.json - the harness executes these, not Claude, so m…
- keybindings-help: Use when the user wants to customize keyboard shortcuts, rebind keys, add chord bindings, or modify ~/.claude/keybindings.json. Examples: "rebind ctrl+s", "add a chord shortcut", "change the submit key", "customize keybindings".
- simplify: Review changed code for reuse, quality, and efficiency, then fix any issues found.
- loop: Run a prompt or slash command on a recurring interval (e.g. /loop 5m /foo, defaults to 10m) - When the user wants to set up a recurring task, poll for status, or run something repeatedly on an interval (e.g. "check the deploy every 5 minutes", "keep…
- schedule: Create, update, list, or run scheduled remote agents (triggers) that execute on a cron schedule. - When the user wants to schedule a recurring remote agent, set up automated tasks, create a cron job for Claude Code, or manage their scheduled agents/…
- claude-api: Build Claude API / Anthropic SDK apps.
TRIGGER when: code imports `anthropic`/`@anthropic-ai/sdk`; user asks to use the Claude API, Anthropic SDKs, or Managed Agents (`/v1/agents`, `/v1/sessions`); or asks to add a Claude feature (prompt caching, ad…
- rbk-prep-release: Prepare release candidate for upstream delivery
- cma-doctor: Validate Concept Model Kit installation
- vvc-BREAK-LOCK: Force-break a stuck VVC lock after a crash.
- cma-normalize: Apply whitespace normalization to concept model documents
- vvc-commit: Guarded git commit
- cma-validate: Validate concept model links and annotations
- cma-render: Transform concept model to ClaudeMark format
- cma-prep-pr: Prepare branch for upstream PR contribution

content.text:
Disk survey (threshold 85%): 228 GB: 78%
Heat: apck-1-proof-of-life (₣A9)
Status: racing
Created: 260410


## Paces

### fixture-walkthrough-debug (₢A9AAK) [rough]

## Character

Guided manual verification. No code changes expected — this is a structured test session using the two synthetic fixtures to confirm the full pipeline works end-to-end on the developer's machine. Debugging character: if something fails, diagnose and fix inline.

## Prerequisites

- App builds clean: `tt/apcw-b.Build.sh` or `cargo build` in apcd/
- Two fixture loaders available: `tt/apcw-fl.FixtureLoad.progress.sh` and `tt/apcw-fl.FixtureLoad.geriatric.sh`

## Walkthrough — Progress Note Fixture

1. Run `tt/apcw-r.Run.sh` to launch the app
2. Run `tt/apcw-fl.FixtureLoad.progress.sh` to load progress note HTML onto clipboard
3. Switch to the app window — triage view should appear
4. Confirm: RED findings include Patient (Margaret Thornton), Attending (Dr. Sarah Whitfield), DOB date, SSN, MRN, phone, email, address, URL
5. Confirm: YELLOW findings include dictionary-matched names in narrative
6. Confirm: DOB shows the date highlighted, not an age yet (age appears in anonymized output)
7. Toggle two YELLOW items to PASS — confirm preview updates (grey styling)
8. Toggle one RED item to PASS — confirm preview updates
9. Click "Copy Anonymized to Clipboard" — button should show "Copied!"
10. Paste into a text editor. Verify:
    - Elided items show category placeholders: `[NAME]`, `[SSN]`, `[PHONE]`, `[EMAIL]`, `[ADDRESS]`, etc.
    - DOB shows `Age: 74` (or appropriate computed age)
    - Items toggled to PASS show original text
    - Line breaks and section structure preserved — readable, not a wall
    - No PHI leaks in elided regions

## Walkthrough — Geriatric Consult Fixture

1. Run `tt/apcw-fl.FixtureLoad.geriatric.sh` to load geriatric HTML onto clipboard
2. Switch to app — triage view should refresh with new content
3. Confirm: clinical content detected, findings populated
4. Click "Copy Anonymized to Clipboard" with all defaults (everything elided)
5. Paste and verify: all PHI replaced, structure preserved
6. If geriatric fixture has a DOB for patient >89 years old, confirm `Age: 90+` appears

## Edge Cases to Probe

- Refocus without changing clipboard — should show "unchanged", triage preserved
- Copy non-clinical text (e.g., "hello world") — should show non-clinical diagnostic, triage cleared
- After non-clinical, click "Copy Anonymized" — should show error (no clinical data)
- Refocus with original fixture again — full triage should reappear

## Exit Criteria

All walkthrough steps pass on both fixtures. Any bugs found are fixed and notched inline before wrapping.

### deploy-and-real-data-test (₢A9AAL) [rough]

## Character

Field deployment and live validation. Ship the app to Ann's MacBook Air, then test against real Epic clipboard content. This is the proof-of-life moment — synthetic fixtures are behind us.

## Deploy

1. Build release: `tt/apcw-b.Build.sh`
2. Deploy to Ann's machine: `tt/apcw-d.Deploy.sh` (scp to `anns-macbook-air:/Users/Shared/apcua/`)
3. If app is already running on Ann's machine, the self-update watcher should detect the new bundle and relaunch automatically
4. If not running, launch manually from `/Users/Shared/apcua/`

## Real Data Test

1. On Ann's machine, open Epic and navigate to a patient chart
2. Use Epic's "Copy All" to copy a clinical note to clipboard
3. Switch to PHI Clipbuddy — triage view should appear
4. Review findings: confirm real patient name, DOB, MRN, provider names detected as RED
5. Review YELLOW findings: check for false positives on common medical terms — these should NOT appear as yellow (whitelist should filter them)
6. Toggle any legitimate non-PHI yellow items to PASS
7. Click "Copy Anonymized to Clipboard"
8. Paste into a text editor — confirm clean output suitable for Open Evidence
9. Paste into Open Evidence and confirm it accepts the text

## What to Watch For

- **False negatives (missed PHI)**: Any patient name, DOB, MRN, or provider name that slips through — critical failure
- **False positives (over-flagging)**: Medical terms, drug names, or common English words flagged as PHI — annoying but fixable via whitelist
- **Formatting**: Is the anonymized output readable? Does it preserve enough structure for Open Evidence to understand?
- **Performance**: Any perceptible lag on focus switch or copy?
- **Self-update**: Did the watcher pick up the new bundle correctly?

## Exit Criteria

At least one real Epic note processed end-to-end: clipboard → triage → toggle → anonymize → paste into Open Evidence. No PHI leaks in anonymized output. Any issues found are documented as itches or new paces.

### ssa-download-resolution (₢A9AAM) [rough]

## Character

Interactive design decision. The premise is clear and the options are enumerated — this pace is a conversation to pick a path, then a small implementation.

## Problem

The `apcad` dictionary refresh binary cannot download SSA Baby Names data programmatically. SSA uses Akamai bot protection that 403s non-browser HTTP clients (ureq, curl). The current workaround requires a human to download `names.zip` in a browser and place it at a staging path (`.ssa_names_staging.zip`). This builds a human into a loop that should be automatable.

## Options Identified

1. **Commit the staging zip** (~7.7MB, public domain). Tool already prefers staging file over download. Eliminates human-in-loop entirely. Update by committing a new zip whenever new year data matters.
2. **Use an alternative mirror** (GitHub, Kaggle, data.gov). Avoids Akamai but trades URL stability.
3. **Browser-mimicking HTTP** (curl-impersonate, crafted TLS). Arms race with Akamai.
4. **Headless browser** (chromiumoxide, playwright). Heavy dependency for one file.
5. **Accept the status quo** — staging zip is a one-time manual step that persists indefinitely. SSA data is historical; new year files are annual.

## Deliverables

- Interactive decision on which option to pursue
- Implement the chosen resolution
- Remove or update the staging fallback messaging if no longer needed

### entity-propagation-tier (₢A9AAN) [rough]

## Character

Focused detection enhancement. Mechanical implementation following established Tier 2/3 patterns — no design ambiguity. The aho-corasick infrastructure already exists for Tier 3; this reuses it with a dynamic phrase dictionary built per-document.

## Problem

Tier 2 (label-anchored) identifies entities like "Maine Medical Center" from the `Facility:` field, but when the same phrase appears in narrative text, no tier catches it. Tier 3 (dictionary) operates on individual words, and common words like "Medical" and "Center" are whitelisted. The identifying unit is the multi-word phrase, not the individual tokens.

## Approach

Add entity propagation between Tier 2 and Tier 3 in `apcre_engine.rs`:

1. After `apcrm_scan_anchored` completes, collect the full multi-word values from labeled field spans (Facility, Patient, Attending, Provider, Referring, etc.)
2. Build a case-insensitive aho-corasick automaton from those phrases
3. Scan `plain_text` for matches — emit RED findings with a new `Propagated` mechanism variant
4. Existing merge logic (`zapcre_merge_findings`) handles overlap with Tier 2's original findings

**Whitespace normalization**: Already handled by the parser (`zapcrp_normalize_whitespace`) — both `plain_text` and span values are single-space-normalized. No additional normalization needed.

**Category**: Inherit the category from the originating Tier 2 finding (Facility stays Facility, Provider stays Provider, etc.)

## Scope

- Modify `apcrm_match.rs`: add `Propagated` to `apcrm_Mechanism` enum
- Modify `apcre_engine.rs`: add propagation scan between Tier 2 and Tier 3 calls
- Add unit tests for propagation (facility in narrative, provider in signature block)
- No frontend changes needed — findings render the same regardless of mechanism

File-touch bitmap (x = pace commit touched file):

  1 A write-specifications
  2 B scaffold-hello-world
  3 C fixture-clipboard-read
  4 D html-parsing
  5 E dictionaries-detection-engine
  6 F frontend-triage-view
  7 G anonymized-output
  8 H self-update-deploy
  9 I dictionary-deepen-from-sources
  10 J dictionary-run-and-debug
  11 K fixture-walkthrough-debug

content.text: Now let me read the gazette for paddock and docket details.

content.content:
1	# jjezs_paddock ₣A9
2	
3	## Character
4	
5	Product design into prototype implementation. Starts with synthesis (specs capturing a rich design conversation), transitions to exploratory engineering (Tauri + Rust + cross-platform clipboard), then steady implementation of a three-tier PHI detection engine. Requires careful attention to minting discipline — this kit lives alongside RBK/JJK/BUK and must respect the prefix universe.
6	
7	## Problem
8	
9	Clinicians using Epic EHR need to consult Open Evidence (an AI diagnostic tool) but must not paste Protected Health Information (PHI) into it. HIPAA Safe Harbor defines 18 PHI categories that must be removed. Today, clinicians either skip the consultation or manually scrub text — both are unacceptable. No clipboard-level tool exists to automate the triage.
10	
11	## Solution
12	
13	Ann's PHI Clipbuddy (`apcap`) — a Tauri desktop app (macOS, later Windows) that:
14	1. Validates clipboard content as clinical via heuristic, clears system clipboard after consumption
15	2. Parses Epic "Copy All" HTML for structural context (labels, sections)
16	3. Runs a three-tier PHI detection engine (regex → label-anchored → dictionary)
17	4. Displays a triage view: RED (definite PHI), YELLOW (questionable), GREY (pass)
18	5. Lets clinician toggle findings — YELLOW easily, RED with deliberate friction (separate panel section)
19	6. Writes anonymized plain text to clipboard with category-specific placeholders (DOB→age, >89→"90+")
20	7. Self-updates from `/Users/Shared/apcua/` without confirmation
21	
22	## Key Design Decisions
23	
24	- **Anchored vs Matched**: Tier 2 (label position) and Tier 3 (dictionary identity) are independent mechanisms with distinct vocabulary — see APCPS
25	- **Case-insensitive**: All dictionary lookups normalize to lowercase; original case preserved in display
26	- **Per-word anchoring**: No multi-word merge — each word individually classified
27	- **Conservative bias**: False positive preferred over false negative
28	- **Provider/facility names**: Treated as PHI (re-identifying in combination with clinical details)
29	- **Clipboard always cleared** after consumption
30	- **Clinical content heuristic** gates processing; non-clinical clipboard shows diagnostic and clears triage
31	
32	## Authoritative Documents
33	
34	| Document | Owns | Path |
35	|----------|------|------|
36	| Product Spec (APCS) | UX plan, workflow, deployment, HIPAA context | `Tools/apck/APCS-Specification.md` |
37	| Prototype Spec (APCPS) | Detection pipeline, tech stack, dictionaries, project structure, scope boundaries | `Tools/apck/APCPS-PrototypeSpecification.md` |
38	| Claude Context | Build/run/deploy instructions, file mappings, prefix tree, tabtargets | `Tools/apck/apck-claude-context.md` |
39	| Rust Coding Guide (RCG) | Naming conventions, test patterns, disciplines | `Tools/vok/vov_veiled/RCG-RustCodingGuide.md` |

41	# jjezs_pace ₢A9AAK
42	
43	## Character
44	
45	Guided manual verification. No code changes expected — this is a structured test session using the two synthetic fixtures to confirm the full pipeline works end-to-end on the developer's machine. Debugging character: if something fails, diagnose and fix inline.
46	
47	## Prerequisites
48	
49	- App builds clean: `tt/apcw-b.Build.sh` or `cargo build` in apcd/
50	- Two fixture loaders available: `tt/apcw-fl.FixtureLoad.progress.sh` and `tt/apcw-fl.FixtureLoad.geriatric.sh`
51	
52	## Walkthrough — Progress Note Fixture
53	
54	1. Run `tt/apcw-r.Run.sh` to launch the app
55	2. Run `tt/apcw-fl.FixtureLoad.progress.sh` to load progress note HTML onto clipboard
56	3. Switch to the app window — triage view should appear
57	4. Confirm: RED findings include Patient (Margaret Thornton), Attending (Dr. Sarah Whitfield), DOB date, SSN, MRN, phone, email, address, URL
58	5. Confirm: YELLOW findings include dictionary-matched names in narrative
59	6. Confirm: DOB shows the date highlighted, not an age yet (age appears in anonymized output)
60	7. Toggle two YELLOW items to PASS — confirm preview updates (grey styling)
61	8. Toggle one RED item to PASS — confirm preview updates
62	9. Click "Copy Anonymized to Clipboard" — button should show "Copied!"
63	10. Paste into a text editor. Verify:
64	    - Elided items show category placeholders: `[NAME]`, `[SSN]`, `[PHONE]`, `[EMAIL]`, `[ADDRESS]`, etc.
65	    - DOB shows `Age: 74` (or appropriate computed age)
66	    - Items toggled to PASS show original text
67	    - Line breaks and section structure preserved — readable, not a wall
68	    - No PHI leaks in elided regions
69	
70	## Walkthrough — Geriatric Consult Fixture
71	
72	1. Run `tt/apcw-fl.FixtureLoad.geriatric.sh` to load geriatric HTML onto clipboard
73	2. Switch to app — triage view should refresh with new content
74	3. Confirm: clinical content detected, findings populated
75	4. Click "Copy Anonymized to Clipboard" with all defaults (everything elided)
76	5. Paste and verify: all PHI replaced, structure preserved
77	6. If geriatric fixture has a DOB for patient >89 years old, confirm `Age: 90+` appears
78	
79	## Edge Cases to Probe
80	
81	- Refocus without changing clipboard — should show "unchanged", triage preserved
82	- Copy non-clinical text (e.g., "hello world") — should show non-clinical diagnostic, triage cleared
83	- After non-clinical, click "Copy Anonymized" — should show error (no clinical data)
84	- Refocus with original fixture again — full triage should reappear
85	
86	## Exit Criteria
87	
88	All walkthrough steps pass on both fixtures. Any bugs found are fixed and notched inline before wrapping.

90	# jjezs_pace ₢A9AAL
91	
92	## Character
93	
94	Field deployment and live validation. Ship the app to Ann's MacBook Air, then test against real Epic clipboard content. This is the proof-of-life moment — synthetic fixtures are behind us.
95	
96	## Deploy
97	
98	1. Build release: `tt/apcw-b.Build.sh`
99	2. Deploy to Ann's machine: `tt/apcw-d.Deploy.sh` (scp to `anns-macbook-air:/Users/Shared/apcua/`)
100	3. If app is already running on Ann's machine, the self-update watcher should detect the new bundle and relaunch automatically
101	4. If not running, launch manually from `/Users/Shared/apcua/`
102	
103	## Real Data Test
104	
105	1. On Ann's machine, open Epic and navigate to a patient chart
106	2. Use Epic's "Copy All" to copy a clinical note to clipboard
107	3. Switch to PHI Clipbuddy — triage view should appear
108	4. Review findings: confirm real patient name, DOB, MRN, provider names detected as RED
109	5. Review YELLOW findings: check for false positives on common medical terms — these should NOT appear as yellow (whitelist should filter them)
110	6. Toggle any legitimate non-PHI yellow items to PASS
111	7. Click "Copy Anonymized to Clipboard"
112	8. Paste into a text editor — confirm clean output suitable for Open Evidence
113	9. Paste into Open Evidence and confirm it accepts the text
114	
115	## What to Watch For
116	
117	- **False negatives (missed PHI)**: Any patient name, DOB, MRN, or provider name that slips through — critical failure
118	- **False positives (over-flagging)**: Medical terms, drug names, or common English words flagged as PHI — annoying but fixable via whitelist
119	- **Formatting**: Is the anonymized output readable? Does it preserve enough structure for Open Evidence to understand?
120	- **Performance**: Any perceptible lag on focus switch or copy?
121	- **Self-update**: Did the watcher pick up the new bundle correctly?
122	
123	## Exit Criteria
124	
125	At least one real Epic note processed end-to-end: clipboard → triage → toggle → anonymize → paste into Open Evidence. No PHI leaks in anonymized output. Any issues found are documented as itches or new paces.

127	# jjezs_pace ₢A9AAM
128	
129	## Character
130	
131	Interactive design decision. The premise is clear and the options are enumerated — this pace is a conversation to pick a path, then a small implementation.
132	
133	## Problem
134	
135	The `apcad` dictionary refresh binary cannot download SSA Baby Names data programmatically. SSA uses Akamai bot protection that 403s non-browser HTTP clients (ureq, curl). The current workaround requires a human to download `names.zip` in a browser and place it at a staging path (`.ssa_names_staging.zip`). This builds a human into a loop that should be automatable.
136	
137	## Options Identified
138	
139	1. **Commit the staging zip** (~7.7MB, public domain). Tool already prefers staging file over download. Eliminates human-in-loop entirely. Update by committing a new zip whenever new year data matters.
140	2. **Use an alternative mirror** (GitHub, Kaggle, data.gov). Avoids Akamai but trades URL stability.
141	3. **Browser-mimicking HTTP** (curl-impersonate, crafted TLS). Arms race with Akamai.
142	4. **Headless browser** (chromiumoxide, playwright). Heavy dependency for one file.
143	5. **Accept the status quo** — staging zip is a one-time manual step that persists indefinitely. SSA data is historical; new year files are annual.
144	
145	## Deliverables
146	
147	- Interactive decision on which option to pursue
148	- Implement the chosen resolution
149	- Remove or update the staging fallback messaging if no longer needed

151	# jjezs_pace ₢A9AAN
152	
153	## Character
154	
155	Focused detection enhancement. Mechanical implementation following established Tier 2/3 patterns — no design ambiguity. The aho-corasick infrastructure already exists for Tier 3; this reuses it with a dynamic phrase dictionary built per-document.
156	
157	## Problem
158	
159	Tier 2 (label-anchored) identifies entities like "Maine Medical Center" from the `Facility:` field, but when the same phrase appears in narrative text, no tier catches it. Tier 3 (dictionary) operates on individual words, and common words like "Medical" and "Center" are whitelisted. The identifying unit is the multi-word phrase, not the individual tokens.
160	
161	## Approach
162	
163	Add entity propagation between Tier 2 and Tier 3 in `apcre_engine.rs`:
164	
165	1. After `apcrm_scan_anchored` completes, collect the full multi-word values from labeled field spans (Facility, Patient, Attending, Provider, Referring, etc.)
166	2. Build a case-insensitive aho-corasick automaton from those phrases
167	3. Scan `plain_text` for matches — emit RED findings with a new `Propagated` mechanism variant
168	4. Existing merge logic (`zapcre_merge_findings`) handles overlap with Tier 2's original findings
169	
170	**Whitespace normalization**: Already handled by the parser (`zapcrp_normalize_whitespace`) — both `plain_text` and span values are single-space-normalized. No additional normalization needed.
171	
172	**Category**: Inherit the category from the originating Tier 2 finding (Facility stays Facility, Provider stays Provider, etc.)
173	
174	## Scope
175	
176	- Modify `apcrm_match.rs`: add `Propagated` to `apcrm_Mechanism` enum
177	- Modify `apcre_engine.rs`: add propagation scan between Tier 2 and Tier 3 calls
178	- Add unit tests for propagation (facility in narrative, provider in signature block)
179	- No frontend changes needed — findings render the same regardless of mechanism

# jjezs_paddock ₣A9

## Character

Product design into prototype implementation. Starts with synthesis (specs capturing a rich design conversation), transitions to exploratory engineering (Tauri + Rust + cross-platform clipboard), then steady implementation of a three-tier PHI detection engine. Requires careful attention to minting discipline — this kit lives alongside RBK/JJK/BUK and must respect the prefix universe.

## Problem

Clinicians using Epic EHR need to consult Open Evidence (an AI diagnostic tool) but must not paste Protected Health Information (PHI) into it. HIPAA Safe Harbor defines 18 PHI categories that must be removed. Today, clinicians either skip the consultation or manually scrub text — both are unacceptable. No clipboard-level tool exists to automate the triage.

## Solution

Ann's PHI Clipbuddy (`apcap`) — a Tauri desktop app (macOS, later Windows) that:
1. Validates clipboard content as clinical via heuristic, clears system clipboard after consumption
2. Parses Epic "Copy All" HTML for structural context (labels, sections)
3. Runs a three-tier PHI detection engine (regex → label-anchored → dictionary)
4. Displays a triage view: RED (definite PHI), YELLOW (questionable), GREY (pass)
5. Lets clinician toggle findings — YELLOW easily, RED with deliberate friction (separate panel section)
6. Writes anonymized plain text to clipboard with category-specific placeholders (DOB→age, >89→"90+")
7. Self-updates from `/Users/Shared/apcua/` without confirmation

## Key Design Decisions

- **Anchored vs Matched**: Tier 2 (label position) and Tier 3 (dictionary identity) are independent mechanisms with distinct vocabulary — see APCPS
- **Case-insensitive**: All dictionary lookups normalize to lowercase; original case preserved in display
- **Per-word anchoring**: No multi-word merge — each word individually classified
- **Conservative bias**: False positive preferred over false negative
- **Provider/facility names**: Treated as PHI (re-identifying in combination with clinical details)
- **Clipboard always cleared** after consumption
- **Clinical content heuristic** gates processing; non-clinical clipboard shows diagnostic and clears triage

## Authoritative Documents

| Document | Owns | Path |
|----------|------|------|
| Product Spec (APCS) | UX plan, workflow, deployment, HIPAA context | `Tools/apck/APCS-Specification.md` |
| Prototype Spec (APCPS) | Detection pipeline, tech stack, dictionaries, project structure, scope boundaries | `Tools/apck/APCPS-PrototypeSpecification.md` |
| Claude Context | Build/run/deploy instructions, file mappings, prefix tree, tabtargets | `Tools/apck/apck-claude-context.md` |
| Rust Coding Guide (RCG) | Naming conventions, test patterns, disciplines | `Tools/vok/vov_veiled/RCG-RustCodingGuide.md` |

# jjezs_pace ₢A9AAK

## Character

Guided manual verification. No code changes expected — this is a structured test session using the two synthetic fixtures to confirm the full pipeline works end-to-end on the developer's machine. Debugging character: if something fails, diagnose and fix inline.

## Prerequisites

- App builds clean: `tt/apcw-b.Build.sh` or `cargo build` in apcd/
- Two fixture loaders available: `tt/apcw-fl.FixtureLoad.progress.sh` and `tt/apcw-fl.FixtureLoad.geriatric.sh`

## Walkthrough — Progress Note Fixture

1. Run `tt/apcw-r.Run.sh` to launch the app
2. Run `tt/apcw-fl.FixtureLoad.progress.sh` to load progress note HTML onto clipboard
3. Switch to the app window — triage view should appear
4. Confirm: RED findings include Patient (Margaret Thornton), Attending (Dr. Sarah Whitfield), DOB date, SSN, MRN, phone, email, address, URL
5. Confirm: YELLOW findings include dictionary-matched names in narrative
6. Confirm: DOB shows the date highlighted, not an age yet (age appears in anonymized output)
7. Toggle two YELLOW items to PASS — confirm preview updates (grey styling)
8. Toggle one RED item to PASS — confirm preview updates
9. Click "Copy Anonymized to Clipboard" — button should show "Copied!"
10. Paste into a text editor. Verify:
    - Elided items show category placeholders: `[NAME]`, `[SSN]`, `[PHONE]`, `[EMAIL]`, `[ADDRESS]`, etc.
    - DOB shows `Age: 74` (or appropriate computed age)
    - Items toggled to PASS show original text
    - Line breaks and section structure preserved — readable, not a wall
    - No PHI leaks in elided regions

## Walkthrough — Geriatric Consult Fixture

1. Run `tt/apcw-fl.FixtureLoad.geriatric.sh` to load geriatric HTML onto clipboard
2. Switch to app — triage view should refresh with new content
3. Confirm: clinical content detected, findings populated
4. Click "Copy Anonymized to Clipboard" with all defaults (everything elided)
5. Paste and verify: all PHI replaced, structure preserved
6. If geriatric fixture has a DOB for patient >89 years old, confirm `Age: 90+` appears

## Edge Cases to Probe

- Refocus without changing clipboard — should show "unchanged", triage preserved
- Copy non-clinical text (e.g., "hello world") — should show non-clinical diagnostic, triage cleared
- After non-clinical, click "Copy Anonymized" — should show error (no clinical data)
- Refocus with original fixture again — full triage should reappear

## Exit Criteria

All walkthrough steps pass on both fixtures. Any bugs found are fixed and notched inline before wrapping.

# jjezs_pace ₢A9AAL

## Character

Field deployment and live validation. Ship the app to Ann's MacBook Air, then test against real Epic clipboard content. This is the proof-of-life moment — synthetic fixtures are behind us.

## Deploy

1. Build release: `tt/apcw-b.Build.sh`
2. Deploy to Ann's machine: `tt/apcw-d.Deploy.sh` (scp to `anns-macbook-air:/Users/Shared/apcua/`)
3. If app is already running on Ann's machine, the self-update watcher should detect the new bundle and relaunch automatically
4. If not running, launch manually from `/Users/Shared/apcua/`

## Real Data Test

1. On Ann's machine, open Epic and navigate to a patient chart
2. Use Epic's "Copy All" to copy a clinical note to clipboard
3. Switch to PHI Clipbuddy — triage view should appear
4. Review findings: confirm real patient name, DOB, MRN, provider names detected as RED
5. Review YELLOW findings: check for false positives on common medical terms — these should NOT appear as yellow (whitelist should filter them)
6. Toggle any legitimate non-PHI yellow items to PASS
7. Click "Copy Anonymized to Clipboard"
8. Paste into a text editor — confirm clean output suitable for Open Evidence
9. Paste into Open Evidence and confirm it accepts the text

## What to Watch For

- **False negatives (missed PHI)**: Any patient name, DOB, MRN, or provider name that slips through — critical failure
- **False positives (over-flagging)**: Medical terms, drug names, or common English words flagged as PHI — annoying but fixable via whitelist
- **Formatting**: Is the anonymized output readable? Does it preserve enough structure for Open Evidence to understand?
- **Performance**: Any perceptible lag on focus switch or copy?
- **Self-update**: Did the watcher pick up the new bundle correctly?

## Exit Criteria

At least one real Epic note processed end-to-end: clipboard → triage → toggle → anonymize → paste into Open Evidence. No PHI leaks in anonymized output. Any issues found are documented as itches or new paces.

# jjezs_pace ₢A9AAM

## Character

Interactive design decision. The premise is clear and the options are enumerated — this pace is a conversation to pick a path, then a small implementation.

## Problem

The `apcad` dictionary refresh binary cannot download SSA Baby Names data programmatically. SSA uses Akamai bot protection that 403s non-browser HTTP clients (ureq, curl). The current workaround requires a human to download `names.zip` in a browser and place it at a staging path (`.ssa_names_staging.zip`). This builds a human into a loop that should be automatable.

## Options Identified

1. **Commit the staging zip** (~7.7MB, public domain). Tool already prefers staging file over download. Eliminates human-in-loop entirely. Update by committing a new zip whenever new year data matters.
2. **Use an alternative mirror** (GitHub, Kaggle, data.gov). Avoids Akamai but trades URL stability.
3. **Browser-mimicking HTTP** (curl-impersonate, crafted TLS). Arms race with Akamai.
4. **Headless browser** (chromiumoxide, playwright). Heavy dependency for one file.
5. **Accept the status quo** — staging zip is a one-time manual step that persists indefinitely. SSA data is historical; new year files are annual.

## Deliverables

- Interactive decision on which option to pursue
- Implement the chosen resolution
- Remove or update the staging fallback messaging if no longer needed

# jjezs_pace ₢A9AAN

## Character

Focused detection enhancement. Mechanical implementation following established Tier 2/3 patterns — no design ambiguity. The aho-corasick infrastructure already exists for Tier 3; this reuses it with a dynamic phrase dictionary built per-document.

## Problem

Tier 2 (label-anchored) identifies entities like "Maine Medical Center" from the `Facility:` field, but when the same phrase appears in narrative text, no tier catches it. Tier 3 (dictionary) operates on individual words, and common words like "Medical" and "Center" are whitelisted. The identifying unit is the multi-word phrase, not the individual tokens.

## Approach

Add entity propagation between Tier 2 and Tier 3 in `apcre_engine.rs`:

1. After `apcrm_scan_anchored` completes, collect the full multi-word values from labeled field spans (Facility, Patient, Attending, Provider, Referring, etc.)
2. Build a case-insensitive aho-corasick automaton from those phrases
3. Scan `plain_text` for matches — emit RED findings with a new `Propagated` mechanism variant
4. Existing merge logic (`zapcre_merge_findings`) handles overlap with Tier 2's original findings

**Whitespace normalization**: Already handled by the parser (`zapcrp_normalize_whitespace`) — both `plain_text` and span values are single-space-normalized. No additional normalization needed.

**Category**: Inherit the category from the originating Tier 2 finding (Facility stays Facility, Provider stays Provider, etc.)

## Scope

- Modify `apcrm_match.rs`: add `Propagated` to `apcrm_Mechanism` enum
- Modify `apcre_engine.rs`: add propagation scan between Tier 2 and Tier 3 calls
- Add unit tests for propagation (facility in narrative, provider in signature block)
- No frontend changes needed — findings render the same regardless of mechanism

content.text:
Here's where **₣A9 apck-1-proof-of-life** stands:

**Progress**: 10 paces wrapped (A through J), 4 remaining — all rough.

**Remaining paces:**

| # | Pace | Character |
|---|------|-----------|
| **₢A9AAK** | fixture-walkthrough-debug | Manual end-to-end walkthrough with synthetic fixtures. You run the app, load fixtures, verify triage/toggle/anonymize. Debug inline if anything fails. |
| **₢A9AAL** | deploy-and-real-data-test | Ship to Ann's MacBook Air, test against real Epic clipboard. The proof-of-life moment. |
| **₢A9AAM** | ssa-download-resolution | Design decision on SSA Baby Names download (Akamai blocks programmatic access). 5 options enumerated, need to pick one. |
| **₢A9AAN** | entity-propagation-tier | Mechanical enhancement — propagate Tier 2 label-anchored entities (facility, provider names) into narrative text via aho-corasick phrase matching. |

**Shape of the remaining work:**
- K and L are human-interactive (you at the keyboard, running the app, observing results)
- M is a short design conversation then small implementation
- N is straightforward Rust implementation following existing patterns

What would you like to focus on?

message.content: What is the master specification for this work?

content.text:
The prototype spec is **APCPS** — `Tools/apck/APCPS-PrototypeSpecification.md`. It owns the detection pipeline, tech stack, dictionaries, project structure, and scope boundaries.

There's also the product spec **APCS** — `Tools/apck/APCS-Specification.md` — which owns UX plan, workflow, deployment, and HIPAA context.

Want me to open one of them?

message.content: We got to the place where the base UI was functionally working, which was excellent.  It was clear some function was starting to happen.  I realized though that we would need to iterate quite a lot, and I don't want to make a screenshot per iteration cycle.  Makes me think we need to devise an alternate invocation pattern for tests, where maybe we pass a filename as an argument, and when we do, the entire clipboard process is bypassed, also don't display window, and instead just write an 'amended' version to a file.  we can iterate well on that.   Thoughts?  For proper debug, I wonder that we need some sort of markup to clearly delineate any replaced text for the reason it was hidden.  We probably also need a metadata file out with the summary of replacements.  Thoughts?

content.text:
This is a strong instinct. The GUI loop is inherently high-friction for iteration — every cycle is: build, launch, load fixture, eyeball triage, click copy, paste, inspect. You can't diff that, you can't regression-test it, and you can't see what the engine *thinks* it's doing.

**The batch binary idea is sound.** You already have the pattern — `apcal` (loader), `apcad` (dictionary refresh) — a third binary like `apcab` (batch) fits naturally in the prefix tree under `apca`. It calls the same library functions (`apcrp_parse`, `apcre_engine`) but skips Tauri entirely.

**On the two output files, I'd suggest:**

**1. Amended text** — the anonymized output but with diagnostic markup instead of bare placeholders:
```
Patient: [RED:PATIENT "Margaret Thornton"]
DOB: [RED:DOB "03/15/1952" → Age: 74]
Facility: [YELLOW:FACILITY "Maine Medical Center"]
The patient presented to [PASS:FACILITY "Maine Medical Center"] with...
```
Severity, category, original text all visible inline. You can read it like a document and immediately see what the engine did and why. Items toggled to PASS (or defaulted PASS) show differently from elided items.

**2. Findings manifest** — structured summary, probably JSON:
```json
{"findings": [
  {"category": "PATIENT", "severity": "RED", "mechanism": "Anchored", "text": "Margaret Thornton", "offset": 42, "length": 17},
  ...
], "stats": {"red": 8, "yellow": 3, "pass": 12}}
```
This is what you diff between runs. Change the engine, re-run, diff the manifests. Positional offsets let you correlate back to the source HTML if needed.

**Design question for you:** In batch mode, what's the toggle policy? The GUI lets humans reclassify YELLOW→PASS. In batch, I'd default to "everything stays as the engine classified it" — no toggling. That way the output is a pure snapshot of engine behavior, which is what you want for iteration. Sound right?

**One more thought:** Since the fixtures are HTML files and you already have them on disk, the invocation could be as simple as:
```
tt/apcw-ba.Batch.sh epic_progress_note.html
```
Writes `epic_progress_note.amended.txt` and `epic_progress_note.findings.json` next to it (or to a designated output dir).

Want to develop this into a pace and get it slated?

message.content:
Yes we will iterate on 'unamended classification' for this aspect of testing.

How do you want to 'write out' the phi elided text?  How indicate an elision?

content.text:
Good — "unamended classification" is a clean mental model. Engine output, no human toggles.

For elision markers, the core tension is: **readable as a document** vs. **greppable as structured data**.

My proposal — single guillemets with severity and category:

```
Patient: ‹RED:PATIENT Margaret Thornton›
DOB: ‹RED:DOB 03/15/1952›
SSN: ‹RED:SSN 123-45-6789›
...presented to ‹YELLOW:FACILITY Maine Medical Center› with complaints of...
Dr. ‹YELLOW:DICTIONARY Johnson› noted...
```

**Why guillemets** (`‹ ›`): Never appear in clinical text. Square brackets do — lab values like `[Na+ 140]`, clinical shorthand like `[patient]`. Guillemets are zero-collision and greppable (`grep '‹' output.txt` finds every elision).

**Why severity:category**: That's the "reason it was hidden." Mechanism (Regex/Anchored/Dictionary) is a second-order debugging concern — belongs in the findings manifest, not inline.

**Format**: `‹SEVERITY:CATEGORY originaltext›` — no extra delimiters inside. Category tells you what kind of PHI; original text lets you judge correctness.

The findings manifest (JSON) would carry the full detail: offset, length, mechanism, severity, category, original text. That's where you do structured diffing between runs.

Does this marker format work for you, or do you want to adjust the information density?

message.content: In that format where you actually 'flow through' the raw phi, I think we can skip the findings manifest.  What if instead of a file, the parameter is a directory, and we just step through all files there and emit to the BUD_OUTPUT_DIR location a file per?  I think we need a good name for this mezzanine raw classification file.

content.text:
Right — the manifest is redundant when the elision markers carry severity, category, and original text inline. One file tells you everything.

Directory-sweep is better than single-file too. Load all `*.html` files from the input dir, emit one output per input. Natural for regression: drop a new fixture in, re-run, diff.

**On the name**: This is a classification snapshot with PHI flowing through in structured markup — not anonymized (PHI is present), not raw (it's been classified). It's a *triage* artifact — the engine's judgment rendered as marked-up text.

Some candidates:

| Name | Feel |
|------|------|
| **triage** | Matches the app's own vocabulary — the triage view, but in text form |
| **assay** | Analytical examination, testing for specific substances (PHI). Clinical resonance. |
| **survey** | Systematic inspection of what's there |
| **census** | Enumeration of everything found |
| **dossier** | Complete file on what was detected |

I'd lean **assay** — it's the right metaphor (testing a sample for known substances), it's short, and it's not overloaded in the codebase. The file would be:

```
epic_progress_note.assay.txt
```

Input dir of `.html` fixtures → output dir of `.assay.txt` files. The extension communicates "this is a classified but not anonymized artifact — it contains PHI."

What do you think?

message.content: That looks good.  Do you see how we run with tabtargets and how that assures an environment variable with a clean output directory in it?

content.text:
Yes. The tabtarget dispatches through the workbench, which calls `buw_kindle` — that sets up `BUW_OUTPUT_DIR` (a clean, timestamped directory under `.buw/`). The command function writes its outputs there. After execution, the workbench opens that directory (or reports the path).

So the tabtarget would be something like:

```
tt/apcw-ba.BatchAssay.sh path/to/fixtures/
```

The folio (input directory) comes via `param1`. The command implementation iterates `*.html` in that directory, runs the detection engine on each, writes `{stem}.assay.txt` files to `BUW_OUTPUT_DIR`. No path juggling — the infrastructure hands you a clean output location.

Want to slate this as a pace?

message.content: I don't think you have _OUTPUT_DIR right, it certainly doesnt appear under .buw; look more closely at bash utility dispatch.

content.content:
Tools/buk/buto_operations.sh:129:#   ZBUTO_BURV_OUTPUT_DIR  - BURV output dir matching BURD_OUTPUT_DIR (empty if BURV not enabled)
Tools/buk/buto_operations.sh:177:  ZBUTO_BURV_OUTPUT_DIR="${z_burv_output:+${z_burv_output}/current}"
Tools/buk/buto_operations.sh:262:  test -n "${ZBUTO_BURV_OUTPUT_DIR:-}" || buto_fatal "No previous tabtarget output"

content.text:
You're right. It's `BURD_OUTPUT_DIR` — set by the dispatch infrastructure in `bud_dispatch.sh`, derived from `BURC_OUTPUT_ROOT_DIR/current`. Cleared and recreated fresh on each tabtarget invocation. The command just writes to `${BURD_OUTPUT_DIR}` — no path management needed.

So the assay command writes `{stem}.assay.txt` files to `${BURD_OUTPUT_DIR}`, and dispatch handles the cleanup/creation lifecycle automatically.

message.content: I am ready to slate a pace first in this heat to add this 'Batch Assay mode' if you are: ask questions if not.  We'll need to add the tabtarget to trigger this on our currently existing .html files.  That is our raw input right?

content.text: Let me confirm where the fixture HTML files live.

Yes — two fixtures in `Tools/apck/test_fixtures/`:
- `epic_progress_note.html`
- `epic_geriatric_consult.html`

Ready to slate. Here's what I'd write:

**Pace silks**: `batch-assay-mode`

**Docket sketch**:
- New binary `apcab` (App Batch) — reads `*.html` from an input directory, runs parse + detection engine, writes `{stem}.assay.txt` to `BURD_OUTPUT_DIR` with guillemet-delimited elision markers (`‹SEVERITY:CATEGORY originaltext›`)
- Tabtarget `tt/apcw-ba.BatchAssay.sh` — folio via `param1` pointing at the fixtures directory
- No Tauri dependency, no clipboard, no window — pure library calls
- Enroll in zipper, wire through workbench

**Placement**: First among remaining paces (before ₢A9AAK fixture-walkthrough-debug), since this gives us the fast iteration loop that all subsequent detection work benefits from.

Shall I slate it?

message.content: Help me decide on new binary versus adding this as a mode in the main application?

content.text:
New binary. Three reasons:

1. **Dependency graph**: `apcap` pulls in Tauri — window system, event loop, clipboard APIs. The batch assay needs none of that. A mode flag means the Tauri dependency compiles and links even when you're just processing files. `apcab` depends only on the library crates (parser, engine, dictionaries).

2. **Existing pattern**: You already have `apcal` (loader) and `apcad` (dictionary refresh) as purpose-built binaries alongside `apcap`. A third fits naturally — the prefix tree has room under `apca`.

3. **Invocation simplicity**: The tabtarget dispatches to a binary. No `--headless` flag, no conditional initialization, no "skip the window if this arg is present" branching inside Tauri startup. The workbench routes to `apcab` directly.

The only argument for a mode would be if batch needed Tauri-specific functionality (like actual clipboard integration). It doesn't — it reads files and writes files.

message.content: All right, lets go with that idea.  Slate the pace now.

input.content:
# jjezs_slate batch-assay-mode

## Character

Focused build-out of a new binary and its tabtarget integration. Mechanical Rust + BUK wiring — the detection engine and parser already exist as library functions. No design ambiguity; the format decisions are made.

## Deliverables

New binary `apcab` (App Batch): reads `*.html` files from an input directory, runs the parse and detection pipeline (apcrp + apcre), writes one `{stem}.assay.txt` per input file to `BURD_OUTPUT_DIR`.

Assay output format — guillemet-delimited elision markers with PHI flowing through:
```
Patient: ‹RED:PATIENT Margaret Thornton›
DOB: ‹RED:DOB 03/15/1952›
...presented to ‹YELLOW:FACILITY Maine Medical Center› with...
```
Unelided text passes through verbatim. Unamended classification — no toggle logic, pure engine output.

## Implementation

- `Tools/apck/apcd/src/apcab_main.rs` — new binary entry point
  - Accept input directory as CLI argument
  - Glob `*.html`, parse each, run detection engine, render assay text
  - Write `{stem}.assay.txt` to `BURD_OUTPUT_DIR`
  - No Tauri dependency — library crates only (apcrp, apcre, apcrm, apcrd)
- Register `apcab` in `Cargo.toml` as a `[[bin]]` entry
- Tabtarget `tt/apcw-ba.BatchAssay.sh` — folio via param1 (input directory path)
- Enroll colophon `apcw-ba` in zipper, wire through workbench
- Update `apck-claude-context.md` prefix tree and file mappings

## Default invocation

```
tt/apcw-ba.BatchAssay.sh Tools/apck/test_fixtures/
```

Emits `epic_progress_note.assay.txt` and `epic_geriatric_consult.assay.txt` to `BURD_OUTPUT_DIR`.

## Exit Criteria

Both fixture HTML files produce assay output. Elision markers present for known PHI in each fixture. Output is readable as a document with classifications visible inline.

content.content: File created successfully at: /Users/bhyslop/projects/rbm_alpha_recipemuster/.claude/jjm/officia/260417-1007/gazette_in.md (file state is current in your context — no need to Read it back)
